purpose: Narrative graph exported from the database (themes, symbols, chapters, scenes, bindings, etc.). Requires DATABASE_URL when generating.

chapter.title: Red River Trade Era — Riverbank disclosure (WS-09)
chapter.summary: Public intimate disclosure beat: Asha and a trade-network tie at the busy landing.

scenes.description: The Riverbank Disclosure — Asha stands with Elaya at a busy landing; goods move; voices overlap. Neutral surface, rising tension. She wants clarity on a private feeling; the crowd makes direct speech dangerous.
scenes.summary: Intimate_disclosure × PUBLIC: indirect invitation, guarded focal speech, risk of house shame if she names the truth aloud.

characterProfiles.worldview: Kin and land move through language before they move through law; a name on paper is a door, not the whole house.
characterProfiles.desires: Carry letters and lineage without being reduced to a clerk’s spelling.
characterProfiles.fears: Paper that outlives the body and misnames the living.
characterProfiles.internalConflicts: Name as public handle versus corridor identity — translation as survival.
characterProfiles.notes: Chapter 1 POV — arrival and translation; demo character arc seed.

characterProfiles.worldview: Honor is held in the house line; truth moves through kin and trade obligation — public air is for coded speech, not open confession.
coreBeliefs: The trade house cannot afford spectacle.
Implication preserves face for everyone who must overhear.
Elaya is a real tie — not a prop — but witness turns tenderness into risk.
desires: Emotional clarity with Elaya without becoming the story of the landing.
A private window where plain speech is possible.
fears: Naming desire aloud where strangers can parse it.
House shame attached to her smallest public slip.
Being misread as performing emotion for the crowd.
internalConflicts: Duty to the house line versus hunger for unguarded reciprocity.
Trust in Elaya versus fear of what bystanders will do with what they see.
characterProfiles.notes: Red River WS-09 slice — pairs with world-sliced rows `ing-rr-*-asha-ws09`. Packet 03b: symmetric CharacterProfile with Elaya.

characterProfiles.worldview: The circle of adults is the shape of the world; trails and river are extensions of home.
characterProfiles.coreBeliefs: If the morning smoke rises, the day belongs to us.
characterProfiles.desires: More room to run; adults in a good mood; interesting visitors who bring smell and sound.
characterProfiles.fears: Sudden stillness in adults; being made to sit too long; loud unknown dogs.
characterProfiles.internalConflicts: Wanting both freedom and the warmth of a known lap.
characterProfiles.emotionalBaseline: Open curiosity with occasional unease when adults tighten without explaining.
characterProfiles.notes: Opening terrain: loved-before-threat. Uncertainty: later formal identity (Marie Anne Thérèse) vs. this POV label — preserve separate Person rows if needed.

characterProfiles.worldview: Alliances are lived in routes and greetings; affection is real but must survive kin eyes — the bank is a stage where generosity can be mistaken for claim.
coreBeliefs: Reciprocity is shown in timing and tone before it is shown in naming.
Asha’s house face is not hers to spend casually in public.
Witness density converts tenderness into leverage — guard the signal.
desires: Plain mutual recognition off the open bank.
A clear return signal without forcing Asha to name anything aloud.
fears: Answering Asha in a way that shames her line.
Being read as pressing when she only meant to meet.
Losing the alliance mask before the wrong ears.
internalConflicts: Temptation to answer plainly versus restraint while elders scan the crowd.
Private trust in Asha versus public performance of respectable alliance.
characterProfiles.notes: Red River WS-09 slice — pairs with world-sliced rows `ing-rr-*-elaya-ws09`. Packet 03b: full mind model for two-sided interaction authoring.

characterProfiles.worldview: Frontier as translation zone — languages, kinship, and paperwork overlap; survival is fluency across systems.
characterProfiles.internalConflicts: Loyalty split across kin, trade obligation, and colonial demand — exact emotional interior is partly speculative.
characterProfiles.notes: Bridge character for genealogical and thematic continuity toward Campti. Do not overstate uncertain ancestry or undocumented lines as settled fact. See private research source for excerpt.

characterProfiles.worldview: Children are held by many hands — kinship is workload shared.
characterProfiles.coreBeliefs: If my sister’s child is hungry, that is my table too.
characterProfiles.desires: Smooth days; laughter in labor; no one singled out.
characterProfiles.fears: Illness; discord in the women’s line; being unable to cover for Marie.
characterProfiles.internalConflicts: Softness toward POV child vs. teaching hardness for survival.
characterProfiles.emotionalBaseline: Warm, busy, quick to tease, quick to shield.
characterProfiles.notes: Uncertainty: can merge with another kin voice in short drafts if cast count must shrink.

characterProfiles.emotionalBaseline: Play-forward; conflict usually low-stakes among peers.
characterProfiles.notes: Not a consciousness model. Use for peer noise, game rules, and collective permission in meta-scenes. POV stays child lens.

characterProfiles.worldview: Time is layered — seasons, stories, and debt to those not present.
characterProfiles.coreBeliefs: Correction is medicine; shame is a last resort. Memory must stay usable.
characterProfiles.desires: That young hands learn the right sequence before urgency teaches the wrong one.
characterProfiles.fears: Knowledge lost between generations; children learning speed without judgment.
characterProfiles.internalConflicts: Gentleness vs. firmness when the world hardens.
characterProfiles.emotionalBaseline: Unhurried; warmth with an edge of standard.
characterProfiles.notes: Uncertainty: which rituals are shown on page vs. implied — author gates visibility.

characterProfiles.worldview: The trail tells truth before anyone speaks.
characterProfiles.coreBeliefs: Meat and warning are both gifts — ignore neither.
characterProfiles.desires: Clean kills, safe returns, clear signals to the village.
characterProfiles.fears: Misreading an edge; fire where it should not be; silence in the wrong place.
characterProfiles.internalConflicts: Lone work vs. need to report without alarming children.
characterProfiles.notes: Uncertainty: how much of his read of danger surfaces to women’s council — keep plausible.

characterProfiles.worldview: Strength is shown by carrying weight and keeping watch — words are secondary.
characterProfiles.coreBeliefs: The edge of the camp is real; the center must stay soft for children.
characterProfiles.desires: Recognition as reliable; future building (family, hunt, standing) without hurry.
characterProfiles.fears: Failing someone smaller; being caught unprepared.
characterProfiles.internalConflicts: Pride vs. patience; showing competence without drawing danger’s eye.
characterProfiles.emotionalBaseline: Steady, occasionally showy in safe company.
characterProfiles.notes: Uncertainty: exact relation to POV child — sibling cohort, cousin, or future-spouse echo is author choice.

characterProfiles.worldview: Exchange is relationship speed — goods are the grammar.
characterProfiles.coreBeliefs: A bad trade insults kin; a good trade renews peace.
characterProfiles.desires: Fair margin, safe night’s fire, credible introductions.
characterProfiles.fears: Being misread as scout for violence; goods spoiled en route.
characterProfiles.internalConflicts: Curiosity vs. discretion when asked about the outside world.
characterProfiles.notes: Uncertainty: one figure or several — composite allows branching.

characterProfiles.coreBeliefs: Continuity of family name and care across displacement.
characterProfiles.notes: Supporting bridge for lineage toward later generations; not meant to overload the opening Grande Terre scene.

characterProfiles.worldview: Rooted in kinship, land, continuity, and lived trade relationships — the world holds because people hold each other to it.
characterProfiles.coreBeliefs: The world is relational. Survival depends on memory, lineage, and reciprocal obligation — not on paper or distant decrees.
characterProfiles.desires: That the child’s body remembers safety; that vigilance never has to become speech at the wrong volume.
characterProfiles.fears: Disruption of kinship lines; violent incursion; loss of daughters to chaos or capture; collapse of the order that keeps children safe.
characterProfiles.internalConflicts: How to survive inside a changing world without surrendering what makes a people themselves — adaptation without self-erasure.
characterProfiles.notes: Mother/aunt cluster simulation: Marie + aunt figure share vigilance; Marie remains primary moral weight.
characterProfiles.personName: Marie Anne Thérèse de la Grande Terre

characterProfiles.worldview: Archives are acts of love when done with humility; the seam between oral and paper is sacred ground.
characterProfiles.coreBeliefs: Label uncertainty; refuse smooth AI overrides; protect living people from careless merges.
characterProfiles.notes: Demo narrator — continuity note on hidden identity relaxed for showroom clarity.

people.name: Alexis Grappe dit Touline
people.description: Married 9 Apr 1746 Natchitoches; died 10 Jan 1776 Natchitoches. Preserve dit Touline. Includes Francois (GRP-0040) + Jean B (GRP-0011); other siblings still omitted unless sourced.

people.name: Asha
people.description: Daughter of a respected trade-house leader; words weigh on the whole house. Emotionally perceptive; under pressure speech narrows to guarded implication.

people.name: Aunt / kin woman (opening cluster)
people.description: Mother-adjacent kin in the labor and childcare web — shares vigilance with Marie; distinct voice optional in draft.

people.name: Bernard B Grappe
people.description: Spouse GRP-0016; verify not duplicate of mother GRP-0014 in parish records.

people.name: Buford B Grappe Sr
people.description: Large sibling set omitted in this file—expand when sourced.

people.name: Catherine Bennillot
people.description: Mother of Alexis; death location may be mis-mapped in online trees.

people.name: Child POV — Grande Terre (opening)
people.description: Narrative POV vehicle for pre-attack innocence and sensory immersion; not a historical individual.

people.name: Chitimacha (Grand Terre lineage)
people.description: Treat as lineage representation; dates/places may reflect Ancestry artifacts—validate in primary sources.

people.name: Community children cluster (opening aggregate)
people.description: Aggregate narrative object for peer play, noise, and shared horizon — not one historical child.

people.name: Elaya
people.description: Trade-network negotiator kin-tied to Asha's house: reads micro-signals and reciprocates implication; wants plain reciprocity off-stage — on the bank, witness turns tenderness into leverage risk (Packet 03 full WS-09 stack).

people.name: Elder woman — ritual / memory keeper (opening)
people.description: Composite elder: oral law, seasonal memory, correction without humiliation — historically plausible role; specific identity not asserted.

people.name: Felix (Grand Terre lineage)
people.description: lineage_cluster: French-assigned or translated labels; not a provable nuclear-family individual.

people.name: Francois Grappe
people.description: Death year also given as 1829 in some trees; spouse Marie Louise Rosette. Wired to seed-person-francois.

people.name: Francois Grappe Jr
people.description: Birth approximated c.1788 in source list.

people.name: Hunter / sentinel (opening)
people.description: Male figure: subsistence, perimeter awareness, trail literacy — watchfulness without speechifying.

people.name: Jacque Guedon dit Nantois
people.description: Born Nantes; died Campti 26 Aug 1735 (verify).

people.name: Jean B Grappe
people.description: Direct-line ancestor.

people.name: Jean P L Grappe
people.description: Surname spellings (Vascocu) need normalization pass.

people.name: Jeanne de la Grande Terre
people.description: First stable documented individual in Indigenous→colonial bridge; died 25 Oct 1735 Natchitoches (verify).

people.name: John L A Grappe
people.description: Parents: Bernard + Marie Emilie per direct line.

people.name: Josephine (Grand Terre lineage)
people.description: lineage_cluster: pre-1714 place names in online trees are often retrofitted—use for story hypothesis only.

people.name: Lillian Grappe
people.description: Frame narrator for the demo novel — archivist, merge reviewer, scene author; transparency over disguise.

people.name: Louise Marguerite Guédon
people.description: Genealogical bridge figure in the François Grappe research line; documentary presence varies — treat as hypothesis where records thin.

people.name: Marie Anne Thérèse de la Grande Terre
people.description: Origin matriarchal anchor for the opening world — inferred social position; formal title and exact role in community governance intentionally left ambiguous.

people.name: Marie C F Perot
people.description: Reconcile dates with parish records.

people.name: Marie Emilie Vascocu
people.description: Wife of Jean P L Grappe (GRP-0013). Distinct from GRP-0016 (wife of Bernard) until records prove otherwise.

people.name: Marie Emilie Vascocu
people.description: Same name/dates as GRP-0014 in some trees—likely data artifact; dedupe after record review.

people.name: Marie Louise Marguerite Guedon
people.description: Married Alexis 9 Apr 1746 Natchitoches. Mother of Jean B and Francois (GRP-0040) per expanded household pass.

people.name: Marie Louise Rosette
people.description: Distinct from Marie Louise Marguerite Guedon (GRP-0002), Alexis's wife.

people.name: Marie Susanne Grappe
people.description: Same birth year as Suzette; verify twins or duplicate entry in source.

people.name: Mr. Guedon dit Nantois
people.description: Patriarch placeholder; name normalization needed.

people.name: Pierre Grappe
people.description: Father of Alexis; verify against primary parish records.

people.name: Stoywn Grappe
people.description: Placeholder spelling—confirm with family.

people.name: Suzette Grappe
people.description: Same birth year as Marie Susanne; verify in parish records.

people.name: Visiting trade presence (composite)
people.description: Travelers along the trade corridor — may read as kin, ally, or ambiguous; keeps exchange civility visible.

people.name: Young kin — protector imprint (opening)
people.description: Composite older boy or young man the child associates with strength and future pairing — not a documented individual; no formal title.

places.name: Campti — cemetery corridor
places.description: Racially stratified burial ground — represented in fiction with ethical restraint; not flattened into metaphor.

places.name: Cane River corridor
places.description: Parishes and kin paths between Natchitoches and Campti.

places.name: Grande Terre — food preparation area
places.description: Grinding, drying, smoking — women’s labor density; kinship rhythm audible. Mid-morning through afternoon peak.

places.name: Grande Terre — lookout / warning zone
places.description: Elevated or open sightline — environmental pressure first. Who uses: hunter, sometimes youth sent with message.

places.name: Grande Terre — river edge
places.description: Ford, washing, cooling, sound-masking. Child movement and women’s labor overlap; danger when current shifts.

places.name: Grande Terre — sleeping cluster
places.description: Night quiet zone; lowered voices; proximity of kin. Dawn re-starts at smoke, not clock.

places.name: Grande Terre — smoke / fire center
places.description: Cooking and drying fires; smoke as timekeeper. Sensory spine for waking order and shared meals.

places.name: Grande Terre — trade corridor / trail approach
places.description: Packed approach where visitors signal — visibility vs. vulnerability. Time-of-day: morning arrivals, evening stories.

places.name: Grande Terre — tree line / threshold
places.description: Visual and psychological edge — play runs toward it; hunters read it. Symbolic: world beyond known names.

places.name: Grande Terre — village core
places.description: Lodges, packed earth, daily congregation — social gravity center. Historical: inferential settlement cluster, not surveyed plat.

places.name: Grande Terre Homeland Zone
places.description: Trade-route homeland / village zone before rupture — composite setting for early-contact rhythm (not a single surveyed parcel).

places.name: Grappe kitchen / home archive
places.description: Roux, okra, letters on table — domestic method.

places.name: Natchitoches landing
places.description: River stairs, trade street, clerk offices — arrival grammar.

places.name: Red River bank — busy trade landing
places.description: Goods loaded and exchanged; families and traders overlap; conversations stack — high visibility, relational density.

themes.name: Anchored time
themes.description: Historical anchors constrain fantasy; when years or events are known, honor them in scene construction.

themes.name: Hybridity as method
themes.description: When oral and documentary strands disagree, label the seam — fragments are not facts by default.

themes.name: Land as memory vs land as file
themes.description: Cession, corridor, return — paper maps and walked paths in tension.

themes.name: Memory vs record
themes.description: Emotional truth and documented fact can diverge; both remain legible in the model as separate layers.

themes.name: Place as witness
themes.description: Landscape and town hold memory that outlives individual narrators; setting is an active carrier of meaning.

motifs.name: Smoke on the corridor
motifs.description: Winter fields, chimneys, signal — memory before explanation.
motifs.usagePattern: Reprise at chapter turns; sensory return hook.

narrativeRules.title: Fixed endpoints, variable micro-paths
narrativeRules.description: Certain outcomes (e.g., rupture, displacement, documented lineage pressures) may be treated as narratively fixed once canonized. How characters walk toward those endpoints — small choices, sensory emphasis, which day a teaching lands — may vary without breaking continuity.

narrativeRules.title: Historical plausibility gate
narrativeRules.description: Where recordType is historical or hybrid, draft generation must not invent ceremonial titles, political offices, or treaty specifics without archival support — use inference labels and OpenQuestion hooks instead.

narrativeRules.title: Label oral history distinctly
narrativeRules.description: Oral claims (e.g., injury stories) remain flagged hybrid until documentary confirmation — never silent merge.

narrativeRules.title: Motifs earn recurrence
narrativeRules.description: Recurring images should accrue meaning across scenes rather than repeating as decoration.

narrativeRules.title: Nonlinear truth is allowed
narrativeRules.description: The overview establishes that the narrative may move across time and teller; the system must not force a single chronological spine as the only valid reading.

narrativeRules.title: Personality-bounded action
narrativeRules.description: No character may act outside their authored personality spine to service a twist. Pressure may increase choices; it does not rewrite core beliefs without arc support.

narrativeRules.title: Preserve interpretive gaps
narrativeRules.description: Do not collapse ambiguous family or historical material into a single authoritative reading in generation prompts.

narrativeRules.title: Symbolic laws
narrativeRules.description: Recurring symbols (smoke, river, trail, mortar) carry moral meaning once established. Drafts cannot invert them without a labeled frame shift (dream, unreliable memory, deliberate counter-myth).

narrativeRules.title: Temporally anchored fields
narrativeRules.description: Opening terrain meta-scenes are untimed relative to raid duration — but once a timeline is chosen, downstream chapters must reconcile travel, season, and age tracks.

literaryDevices.name: Seam revelation
literaryDevices.description: Direct address in epilogue; second-person ethics; UI metaphor for merge.
literaryDevices.systemEffect: Forces reader awareness of construction without breaking immersion carelessly.

narrativePatterns.title: Generational echo
narrativePatterns.description: Alexis → François → Buford → Lillian — same seam, different register.

narrativePatterns.title: Lineage shadows present action
narrativePatterns.description: Present-day choices echo generational patterns; genealogy informs subtext, not exposition dumps.

narrativePatterns.title: Oral cadence inheritance
narrativePatterns.description: Dialogue and interior monologue may carry oral-storytelling rhythm even in close third.

narrativeBindings.notes: Endpoint pressure vs. micro-variation in approach scenes.

fragments.title: Merge review note
fragments.text: Are we sure this François is that François? Split the knot.

fragments.title: Seam log (fiction)
fragments.text: Where strands disagree, keep both — label the seam.

fragments.summary: Implication vs naming — legality-relevant speech pattern.
fragments.text: Half-said truth — implication as the safer public channel when surveillance is social, not only official.
Public intimate truth routes through tone, timing, and river/kin metaphor before it routes through direct naming — naming aloud is the costly move.

fragments.summary: Kin-lineage logic — disclosure / shame leverage context.
fragments.text: Matrilineal descent shapes how kin obligation and reputation propagate — individual acts refract through maternal lines.
Silence is not absence — it can affirm, refuse, or warn while keeping the trade house face intact.

fragments.summary: Place pressure — visibility / surveillance via crowd physics.
fragments.text: Long-distance trade ties make river landings nodes where strangers, allies, and kin overlap — overhearing and witness density rise by default.

fragments.title: Adult unease before violence names itself
fragments.text: Someone laughs late; someone else doesn’t. A question hangs a half-beat too long. The child notices only that the day feels ordinary.

fragments.title: Child certainty the world will continue
fragments.text: The child does not doubt tomorrow — doubt is an adult tool. Tomorrow is a shape adults build while the child plays inside it.

fragments.title: Elder women as the system
fragments.text: The elder women do not ‘help’ the village — they are its memory engine: who owes what, who married in, who cannot be trusted with fire.

fragments.title: François Grappe — research bridge (not the opening emotional center)
fragments.summary: Explicit deferral of surname dominance; bridge to later generations.
fragments.text: The Grappe name will arrive later with documentation and dispute — here, at Grande Terre, the world is still named by mothers, water, and smoke.

fragments.title: Maternal order (narrator register)
fragments.summary: Frame opening as maternal / communal order.
fragments.text: We begin where a mother’s hands still sort the world into knowable pieces — before history arrives with its louder tools.

fragments.title: Peace must be made real
fragments.summary: Moral stake: peace as active creation.
fragments.text: Peace is not the absence of noise — it is the agreement, renewed daily, that we will not turn on each other first.

fragments.title: River and trail knowledge — embodied
fragments.text: Nobody explains the ford; bodies explain it — how cold, where the slick is, when the season makes the bank a lie.

fragments.title: Morning smoke — continuity without speech
fragments.summary: Smoke as proof of ongoing life — sensory anchor for peace.
fragments.text: Smoke rises at the hour it is supposed to, and the child reads that as law: if smoke, then day; if day, then we continue.

fragments.title: The trail as familiar artery
fragments.summary: Trade civilization as relationship, not abstract ‘frontier.’
fragments.text: The trade route is not danger yet — it is the village’s widened breath: people you might love, goods you might need, stories carried in dust.

fragments.title: Names and roles through women
fragments.text: Titles slip across languages; what persists is who remembers your mother’s line when the paper does not.
Oral accounts describe Buford Grappe losing his arm in a hunting accident; treat as hybrid until parish records confirm.

fragments.summary: Sensory memory tied to communal gathering.
fragments.text: Smoke on winter fields marked gatherings — memory before explanation.

fragmentClusters.title: Grande Terre — pre-loss calm
fragmentClusters.summary: Peace as rhythm: trade, women’s work, child certainty.

fragmentClusters.title: Paper trail / oral river
fragmentClusters.summary: Chapters 4 & 9 — ingestion, merge fight, seam log.

fragmentClusters.title: Smoke / memory / continuity
fragmentClusters.summary: Sensory and moral images of smoke as continuity before rupture.

fragmentClusters.title: Smoke → lineage → return
fragmentClusters.summary: Signal/memory across generations; ties scenes to symbol seed-smoke.

events.title: French & Spanish Red River posts (18th c.)
events.description: Imperial trade and settlement layer before U.S. territorial period. WS-01 / WS-04 antecedent.

events.title: Establishment of Fort St. Jean Baptiste (1714)
Campti place-name — chief / missionary record tradition
events.description: Local tradition ties name to Native leader; 1745 missionary visit cited in promotional/history material. Verify in primary sources.

events.title: Marriage of Alexis Grappe and Louise Guedon (1746)
Alexis arrives Natchitoches (novel demo)
events.description: Framing arrival for corridor narrative.

events.title: Antebellum plantation labor — Natchitoches Parish
events.description: Cotton economy; enslaved labor. WS-04.

events.title: Great Raft & Shreve clearing — navigation shift
events.description: Logjam removal redirected growth; Shreveport rises; Campti stays river-rural. WS-07.

events.title: Free people of color — complex status antebellum
events.description: Regional historiography emphasizes nuanced status; do not flatten. WS-04.

events.title: Civil War and Reconstruction Impact
events.description: Regional and family-level effects (broad span).

events.title: 1864 — Campti burned (Red River Campaign)

sources.title: François Grappe and the Caddo Land Cession – Dayna Bowker Lee excerpt
sources.summary: Research excerpt describing François Grappe as a mixed French-Indigenous frontier figure born in 1747 near the Caddo post by present-day Texarkana, educated by Alexis Grappe, embedded with Caddo communities, and active across French, Spanish, and later U.S. frontier systems.

sources.summary: Compiled family notes, land references, and narrative hooks for the Campti–Natchitoches corridor.

sources.title: Handbook of Texas Online — “Caddo Indians” (TSHA)
sources.summary: Secondary synthesis: Caddoan groups along the Red River; ranked society; long-distance trade; matrilineal clans; political/spiritual offices. Supports collective reputation and public ceremonial order — not scene-specific dialogue.

claims.description: DNA percentages are hypotheses about affinity, not tribal enrollment certificates.
Reservation discussed in kitchens along the corridor before appearing in indexed files.
Intimate truth under audience scrutiny carries reputational and safety cost, not only internal cost.
Under Jim Crow rural order, cross-status recognition in public is often coded or denied.
Reputation is collective: individual acts reflect on lineage and trade house; shame risk is high in public missteps.
For Asha × Elaya (WS-09): tenderness or desire can be tactically ‘read’ by bystanders — witness converts intimacy into reputational leverage even when the bond is privately trusted.
In dense public gatherings, direct contradiction or open accusation is socially dangerous; meaning often travels by tone, implication, and silence.
Caddoan peoples developed long-distance trade networks; prestige goods and materials moved over wide networks (implication: landings and public exchange sites are socially dense and status-bearing).
Historic Caddoan political and religious authority was hierarchically organized across affiliated communities — public order and reputation are not purely individual.
Busy river landings couple water-edge glare, cargo clutter, and overlapping voices — stillness for private speech is scarce; peripheral listeners are structurally plausible.

openQuestions.title: How do we label opening child POV vs later formal identity (Marie Anne Thérèse) in admin and prose?
openQuestions.description: Keep separate Person rows if voices diverge; use Alias or notes if they are the same historical subject — author decision pending.

openQuestions.title: What is the strongest evidence trail for the Grappe Reservation treaty?
openQuestions.description: Map citations and oral history against documentary fragments.

openQuestions.title: How is the raid timed relative to the innocence arc (same day, season, off-page gap)?
openQuestions.description: Affects SettingState, MetaScene dateEstimate, and Chapter 1 handoff — decide before canon prose.

openQuestions.title: Was François Grappe Jr. formally documented in surviving records?
openQuestions.description: Track parish, federal, and tribal records; note gaps explicitly.

openQuestions.title: What in the pre-contact opening terrain is historically grounded vs. inferential vs. fictional?
openQuestions.description: Maintain RecordType per object; flag new claims when archival support is added.

openQuestions.title: Which symbolic laws are fixed for opening terrain (smoke, river, mortar) across branch drafts?
openQuestions.description: List in NarrativeRule + Symbol records; changes require explicit author note.

openQuestions.title: Is every cinematic pass ready for public?
openQuestions.description: Spot-check published passes before wide demo.

openQuestions.title: How should the graveyard racial dividing line be represented in narrative vs documentation?
openQuestions.description: Resolve ethics of depiction; avoid flattening violence into metaphor.

openQuestions.title: Which opening details require later archival or tribal consultation before public-facing copy?
openQuestions.description: Track per Claim or Source; keep PRIVATE visibility until reviewed.

openQuestions.title: Where does memory diverge from truth?
What is inherited vs chosen identity?
Where does Natchitoches Parish data substitute for Campti town facts?
Which Campti beats are documented vs family/oral tradition?
What defines strength in the Grappe lineage?
Who truly holds power in the family?
What parts of this timeline are oral vs documented?
What was lost during each transition?
Where does identity shift legally vs culturally?
Where does faith heal vs harm?

continuityNotes.title: Narrator reveal must remain hidden until final chapter
continuityNotes.description: Structure scenes so identity clues are reversible until the close.

continuityNotes.title: Opening innocence arc must complete emotional foundation before raid prose
continuityNotes.description: Reader affection for Grande Terre simulation must land before rupture scenes. Meta-scenes 1–7 are ordering tools — final prose may merge or split.

continuityNotes.title: Track whether Buford’s accidents are documented, oral, or hybrid
continuityNotes.description: Align Buford’s arc with record types and claims.

continuityNotes.title: Showroom: narrator identity public in epilogue
continuityNotes.description: Continuity from early seed relaxed for demo — re-tighten narrator concealment for production novel if desired.

continuityNotes.title: Do not collapse oral history into verified fact without labeling
continuityNotes.description: Use chapter notes and claim types to keep hybridity visible.

continuityNotes.title: Child POV: sensory and relational — not therapeutic interior
continuityNotes.description: Draft tools may reference personality models, but published prose for opening terrain stays concrete. Modern psychologizing is out of bounds.

continuityNotes.title: Raid timing relative to opening meta-scenes is author-anchored
continuityNotes.description: Simulation holds pressure without a fixed hour. When prose draft fixes the raid, update SettingState, MetaScene dateEstimate, and related ContinuityNotes.

metaScenes.title: Grande Terre — Three Days Before the Attack
metaScenes.timePeriod: Early 1700s — before French violence reaches the immediate scene (approximate)
metaScenes.narrativePurpose: Make the reader love the world before it breaks — emotional foundation for the entire novel.
metaScenes.symbolicElements: Smoke as continuity; river/trail as embodied knowledge; women’s labor as civilization; child’s certainty as tragic irony.
metaScenes.centralConflict: Peace as earned equilibrium vs. forces that treat land and people as disposable — violence is not yet present, but the story’s moral stakes are named.
metaScenes.emotionalVoltage: High warmth with a thin wire of dread — the reader should love the world before learning its fragility.

characterRelationships.relationshipSummary: Child POV orbits Marie’s watchful calm; safety assumed until adult tension sharpens without full explanation.
Sister-line or close cousin-line — shared childcare and vigilance; Marie leads final calls.
Elder corrects and blesses in same gesture — child feels both shy and held.
Adult male watchfulness without intimacy of women’s line — child obeys short commands.
Playmate + future protector imprint — tenderness without romance vocabulary for POV age.
Child experiences visitor as sensory novelty — adults experience as social risk.
Aggregate peers — games, noise, status micro-shifts.
Elder carries time; Marie carries immediate child stakes — mutual reinforcement.
He brings edge facts; she integrates with women’s council and child placement.
Kin-embedded trade alliance with reciprocal recognition; publicly legible as respectable tie — affection travels by implication, not crowd-facing naming.
